sections.heading: Anatomy and Function
sections.content: The thyroid gland is a bi-lobed endocrine organ located in the anterior neck, overlying the trachea and larynx, and is derived embryologically from the floor of the pharynx (thyroglossal duct). The primary function of thyroid follicular cells is the synthesis, storage, and secretion of thyroid hormones, while parafollicular C cells produce calcitonin.

sections.heading: Definition and Classification
sections.content: Thyroid carcinoma is a malignancy arising from the follicular cells or parafollicular C cells of the thyroid gland. Thyroid carcinomas are classified based on their cell of origin, histological features, and molecular alterations.

sections.heading: Histological Subtypes
sections.content: The main types include differentiated thyroid carcinomas (papillary, follicular, and oncocytic), which originate from follicular cells and often retain the ability to concentrate iodine; medullary thyroid carcinoma, arising from neuroendocrine C cells; and anaplastic (undifferentiated) thyroid carcinoma, a highly aggressive tumor that may arise from dedifferentiation of a pre-existing differentiated carcinoma [ST-1, MEDU-1, ANAP-1].
